text.title: Cache Key Design, TTL, and Common Problems
text.summary: Rules for cache key construction, TTL guidelines per data type, hit-ratio targets, and solutions to the six most common cache failure modes: stampede, hot keys, cold start, stale data, memory pressure, and inconsistency.

rule.statement: Use hierarchical, predictable key patterns. Include version suffix (v1, v2) for keys tied to a schema; never include unstable attributes (timestamps, request IDs, nonces) in keys.
rule.rationale: Predictable keys allow prefix-based invalidation (SCAN + DEL pattern), version suffixes enable cheap schema-change rollover without manual key purge.

list.item: Simple entity: user:{user_id} — maps directly to a single record.
Nested entity: user:{user_id}:orders:page:{page} — hierarchical for pagination.
With version: user:{user_id}:profile:v2 — bump v2 after schema change; old v1 keys expire via TTL.
Complex parameters: search:{md5(sorted_query_params)} — hash to bound key length; sort params first for determinism.
Forbidden in keys: timestamps, request IDs, random nonces, user-agent strings — these produce unique keys on every call, making the cache useless.

rule.statement: Never cache user-specific data with a global key. Always include the user or tenant identifier in the key.
rule.rationale: Global keys for personalized data cause cross-user data leaks; this is a security bug, not just a stale-data bug.

rule.statement: Always add jitter to TTLs (base * (1 + rand(-0.1, 0.1))) to avoid synchronized expiry storms across horizontally scaled instances.
rule.rationale: Without jitter, all instances that populated the cache at the same time expire simultaneously, causing a thundering herd on the DB.

list.item: Static assets (CSS/JS): 1 year — versioned filenames ensure cache busting on deploy.
Product catalog: 5–15 min — moderate change frequency; stale for seconds is acceptable.
User profiles: 1–5 min — personal data, changes occasionally, staleness is visible to the user.
Session data: 30 min–24h — security vs UX balance; sliding expiry on activity.
Search results: 1–5 min — freshness matters, cache to absorb repeated identical queries.
Configuration / feature flags: 1–5 min — short TTL prevents stale config from causing outages.
Real-time data: 10–60 sec — near real-time; cache only if DB/API pressure is demonstrated.
Auth tokens / permissions: match the token TTL — never cache longer than the token is valid.

section.title: Hit-Ratio Targets

list.item: CDN static assets: >95% — anything below indicates poor cache-control headers or key fragmentation.
API responses: >70% — baseline target; below this the cache rarely pays back its operational cost.
Database query cache: >80% — significant load reduction; below 60% the cache is adding latency without benefit.
Session data: >90% — user-specific, near-deterministic access; low ratio indicates short TTL or key churn.

section.note: Track hit ratio per key prefix, not in aggregate. An overall 80% can hide a dead cache on a hot path masking a burned-out 20% of traffic.

rule.statement: Cache stampede (thundering herd) must be mitigated with single-flight / request coalescing, not solely with probabilistic early expiration. Both defenses are needed at scale.
rule.rationale: XFetch (probabilistic early expiration) reduces stampede probability but does not eliminate it at high concurrency; single-flight collapses concurrent misses to one upstream fetch.

list.item: Cache Stampede: multiple processes attempt to rebuild the same cache entry simultaneously on expiry. Solutions: single-flight (collapse concurrent misses), probabilistic early expiration (XFetch), distributed lock (SETNX + TTL) during fill.

Hot Keys: single key receives disproportionate read traffic, pinning one Redis shard. Solutions: local in-process L1 cache in front of Redis, key replication to N shards with random suffix selection, read-aside from replicas.

Cold Start: cache empty after deploy or failover; all requests hit DB. Solutions: cache warming script runs before traffic is routed, gradual traffic shift (canary) during startup, pre-populate from a recent dump.

Stale Data: TTL too long or event invalidation missed. Solutions: shorten TTL, implement reliable CDC-based event invalidation (Debezium / outbox pattern), version-based keys for schema-driven invalidation.

Memory Pressure: cache approaching maxmemory limit, triggering evictions that hurt hit ratio. Solutions: set explicit eviction policy (allkeys-lru or volatile-ttl), monitor eviction rate (not just memory %), tier cold data to a cheaper store.

Inconsistency: write-through inconsistency between cache and DB. Solutions: use distributed transactions (rare), accept eventual consistency with bounded TTL, use version keys so stale data auto-expires.

Stale Negatives: caching "not found" causes ghost entries after a subsequent write creates the record. Solution: choose explicitly whether to cache misses; if yes, use very short TTL (5–30 sec) and invalidate on create.
